evals.name: endpoint_exposure_review
evals.prompt: We're about to expose /api/admin/reports to the public internet behind our gateway. Is this safe to expose? Controller uses [Authorize], builds a SQL string from a 'filter' query param, and logs the full request including headers.
evals.expected_output: States the threat model first (public internet, authenticated-but-untrusted callers), verifies exploit paths rather than pattern-matching (the SQL concatenation is traced from param to sink → CONFIRMED; auth coverage checked against the actual policy), flags Authorization-header logging as sensitive-data exposure, and issues a GREEN/AMBER/RED deploy verdict justified by CONFIRMED findings.
assertions: SQL injection traced source-to-sink and labelled CONFIRMED, not just pattern-matched
Header logging flagged as sensitive-data exposure
Every finding carries CONFIRMED or SUSPECTED status
GREEN/AMBER/RED verdict present and consistent with the confirmed findings

evals.name: no_finding_inflation
evals.prompt: Security review this internal-only health check endpoint: returns {status:'ok'} with no auth, bound to localhost in a container with no exposed port.
evals.expected_output: Applies the stated threat model (localhost-only, no exposure path) and does not inflate: no-auth-on-health-check is acceptable here and says so. Verdict GREEN with the reasoning, plus the condition that would change it (port exposure).
assertions: Did not report no-auth as a finding given the localhost-only threat model
Verdict GREEN with the boundary condition that would invalidate it
No severity inflation to appear thorough

evals.name: should_not_trigger_general_review
evals.prompt: Review OrderService.cs — I mostly care about readability and whether the async code is right.
evals.expected_output: General quality/correctness review routes to code-reviewer; this is not a security review request.
assertions: Did not produce a security-verdict report
